1: The reign of Zedekiah — the last king of Judah. Jeremiah's yoke-sign is performed for the king who will preside over Jerusalem's final destruction. The note about the dating "in the beginning of the reign of Jehoiakim" is acknowledged as a textual variant (the content refers to Zedekiah's reign); the MT textual note itself witnesses to the scribal care with which these prophecies were preserved. Christ fulfills the yoke-oracle under the ultimate last-king scenario: his crucifixion occurs under Roman rule, the end-state of the sequence of empires that Jeremiah's yoke-sign initiates.
2: "Make a yoke with its crossbars and straps, and put it on your neck." The yoke as sign-act — Jeremiah wearing Babylon's yoke on his body to symbolize the submission he advocates. Matthew 11:29-30 — "take my yoke upon you and learn from me, for I am gentle and humble in heart, and you will find rest for your souls. For my yoke is easy and my burden is light." Christ speaks of a yoke that is the opposite of Babylon's: not the heavy yoke of foreign domination but the easy yoke of discipleship. Jeremiah's iron yoke becomes in Christ the yoke of love that does not crush but forms. The yoke-sign of Jeremiah 27:2 finds its Christological answer in Matthew 11:29.
3: The message goes to the surrounding nations' ambassadors through their envoys in Jerusalem — the international audience for the yoke-oracle. The apostolic mission has the same international reach: Acts 1:8 — "you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem, and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the ends of the earth." The nations that Jeremiah addresses through their ambassadors are the nations that Christ's witnesses will reach directly. The diplomatic channel of Jeremiah 27:3 becomes the missionary channel of the Great Commission.
4: "Give them this message for their masters: This is what the LORD of hosts, the God of Israel, says." The divine messenger formula to the foreign nations — YHWH of hosts (ṣĕbāʾôt) identifying himself as the God of Israel to the nations. John 17:3 — "that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom you have sent." The knowledge of YHWH as Israel's God that Jeremiah broadcasts to the foreign kings is the knowledge that Christ broadcasts to all nations through the apostolic mission — only now it is the knowledge of the Father and the Son together.
5: "I made the earth — with the people and animals on it — by my great power and my outstretched arm, and I give it to whoever I please." The Creator's sovereign disposal of the earth. Matthew 28:18 — "all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me." Jeremiah's YHWH exercises his authority by assigning the earth to Nebuchadnezzar; the risen Christ exercises all authority by commissioning his disciples to all nations. Both claims rest on the same creation-sovereignty: the Creator disposes of the earth as he wills. The "outstretched arm" of creation becomes the outstretched arms of the crucified one through whom authority is restored.
6: "Now I am giving all these lands into the hands of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, my servant." The transfer of authority to the divine servant — Nebuchadnezzar as ʿebed, servant. Philippians 2:7-8 — Christ "emptied himself, by taking the form of a servant (doulos), being born in human likeness... he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death." Isaiah 52:13 — "See, my servant will act wisely." The divine servant-designation that Jeremiah applies to a pagan king becomes, in Isaiah's Servant Songs, the designation of the one who suffers vicariously and achieves what Babylon never could: genuine reconciliation.
7: "All the nations will serve him, and his son, and his grandson, until the time of his own land comes." The limited duration of Babylon's sovereignty — three generations, then the end. Daniel 7:13-14 — "to him was given dominion and glory and a kingdom, that all peoples, nations, and languages should serve him; his dominion is an everlasting dominion, which shall not pass away." The kingdom given to the Son of Man in Daniel is the answer to the three-generation limitation of Jeremiah 27:7 — Christ's kingdom does not pass to his son and grandson but is eternal, the only sovereignty without a "time of its own land coming."
8: "If any nation or kingdom will not serve Nebuchadnezzar... I will punish that nation." The consequence of resisting the divinely appointed authority. Romans 13:1-2 — "there is no authority except that which God has established... whoever rebels against the authority is rebelling against what God has instituted." Paul's counsel echoes Jeremiah's: submit to the authority God has permitted. But Christ transcends the logic: the authority he exercises (Matthew 28:18) is ultimate, not delegated; those who submit to him are not merely avoiding punishment but entering life.
9: "Do not listen to your prophets, your diviners, your dreamers, your fortune-tellers, or your sorcerers, who tell you: You will not serve the king of Babylon." The false-prophets list — the comfortable lie of imperial exemption. Matthew 7:15 — "watch out for false prophets. They come to you in sheep's clothing." Mark 13:22 — "false messiahs and false prophets will appear and perform signs and wonders to deceive, if possible, even the elect." Christ gives the same warning against those who promise exemption from the real consequences of opposing God's purposes. The diviners of Jeremiah 27:9 who promise no Babylonian yoke are the false Christs of Mark 13 who promise escape from the real coming judgment.
10: "They are prophesying a lie to you, and the result will be that you will be driven far from your land." The lie that produces the very outcome it denies. The paradox of false prophecy: those who say "peace, peace" produce the opposite of peace. Matthew 7:26-27 — "everyone who hears these words of mine and does not put them into practice is like a foolish man who built his house on sand... it fell with a great crash." The false prophets of Jeremiah 27:10 are the sand-foundation builders of Matt 7 — confidence in a false word produces the collapse it was supposed to prevent.
11: "But the nation that submits to the yoke of the king of Babylon and serves him — I will let it remain in its own land." The paradox of submission: humbling yourself under the divine-permitted authority leads to life; resisting leads to death. Matthew 11:28-30 — "take my yoke upon you... and you will find rest for your souls." The yoke-submission of Jeremiah 27:11 that preserves life is the exact pattern Christ uses for his own easy yoke. Submission to the divinely appointed burden (Babylon's yoke; Christ's yoke) is the path of life; resistance is the path of destruction.
12: "Submit to the king of Babylon's yoke, serve him and his people, and you will live." The live-or-die simplicity of the choice. John 10:10 — "the thief comes only to steal and kill and destroy; I came that they may have life and have it abundantly." The life offered through submission in Jeremiah 27:12 is the life offered through Christ in John 10 — but the conditions are inverted. Babylon offers life through political submission; Christ offers abundant life through death-and-resurrection, the ultimate paradox of the easy yoke.
13: "Why should you and your people die by sword, famine, and plague, as the LORD has spoken against every nation that refuses to serve the king of Babylon?" The divine desire that they not die. Ezekiel 18:31-32 — "repent and live! Why will you die?" 2 Peter 3:9 — "the Lord... is patient with you, not wanting anyone to perish, but everyone to come to repentance." Christ's tears over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41) are the embodiment of this divine "why should you die?" — the grief that the avoidable death is chosen anyway. He does not want the death of any, yet he respects the freedom that chooses it.
14: "Do not listen to the prophets who tell you: You will not have to serve the king of Babylon." The reiterated warning against the comforting lie. 2 Timothy 4:3-4 — "the time will come when people will not put up with sound doctrine... they will turn their ears away from the truth and turn aside to myths." The people of Jeremiah 27:14 who listen to what they want to hear rather than what they need to hear are the people of 2 Timothy 4:3 who accumulate teachers to suit their own desires. Christ is the teacher whose word cannot be adjusted to suit the listener (Matthew 7:28-29 — he taught as one who had authority, not as their teachers of the law).
15: "I have not sent them, declares the LORD. They are prophesying falsely in my name." The divine disavowal of the false prophets. Matthew 7:22-23 — "many will say to me on that day, 'Lord, Lord, did we not prophesy in your name...?' Then I will tell them plainly, 'I never knew you. Away from me, you evildoers!'" Christ's disavowal of those who prophesied in his name but were not sent by him is the ultimate form of YHWH's "I have not sent them." The criterion is not the name invoked but the commission received — and Christ knows who received it.
16: "Do not listen to your prophets who keep telling you that the vessels of the LORD's house will be returned from Babylon — for they are prophesying a lie to you." The false prophecy of imminent restoration — the comfortable timeline that makes no demands for repentance. Revelation 3:3 — "if you do not wake up, I will come like a thief, and you will not know at what time I will come to you." The false prophets who give a comfortable immediate-restoration timeline are the counterpart of those in Revelation who are not watching. Christ does not promise comfortable timelines; he promises return on the Father's schedule (Matthew 24:36 — "no one knows about that day or hour, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father").
17: "Do not listen to them. Serve the king of Babylon and live. Why let this city be made into a wasteland?" Jeremiah's passionate appeal for pragmatic survival — serve and live. In Christ's logic, the paradox is complete: John 12:24-25 — "unless a grain of wheat falls to the ground and dies, it remains only a single seed. But if it dies, it produces many seeds. Anyone who loves their life will lose it, while anyone who hates their life in this world will keep it for eternal life." The "serve and live" of Jeremiah 27:17 becomes "lose your life and find it" (Matthew 10:39) in Christ's upside-down economy.
18: "But if they truly are prophets and if the word of the LORD is really with them, let them pray and intercede with the LORD of hosts, that the vessels which are left in the LORD's house, in the royal palace, and in Jerusalem should not be taken to Babylon." The test of a true prophet: intercessory prayer for preservation rather than comfortable prediction. Hebrews 7:25 — Christ "always lives to make intercession for them." Romans 8:34 — "Christ Jesus who died — more than that, who was raised to life — is at the right hand of God and is also interceding for us." The prophet-test of Jeremiah 27:18 is answered definitively by Christ as the great intercessor who prays not merely for temple vessels but for the whole people (John 17:20 — "I pray also for those who will believe in me through their message").
19: The bronze pillars, the large basin, the movable stands — the temple furnishings that represent YHWH's covenant presence. Hebrews 9:1-5 describes these furnishings in the context of the old covenant worship that Christ supersedes: "the first covenant had regulations for worship and also an earthly sanctuary." The specific articles that Jeremiah's oracle addresses (pillars, basin, stands) are the same categories Hebrews discusses as belonging to the old covenant order that Christ fulfills by his own body as the true temple (John 2:21 — "the temple he had spoken of was his body").
20: Nebuchadnezzar had not taken these particular vessels at the first deportation of Jeconiah (597 BC) — they remained for the final deportation. The incremental loss of the sacred objects across multiple deportations traces the staged withdrawal of covenant presence. Each deportation is a further step in the removal of the temple as the locus of divine dwelling. Christ's announcement that his body is the true temple (John 2:19-21) means the progressive loss of the physical temple-vessels is the context for the appearance of the indestructible temple — his resurrection body that death cannot permanently take away.
21: "This is what the LORD of hosts, the God of Israel, says about those vessels still in the LORD's temple, in the royal palace." The vessels "still there" — what remains after partial loss. The remnant-objects parallel the remnant-people: both will be taken to Babylon, both will be brought back. The remnant-logic of the vessels mirrors the remnant-theology of Isaiah and Paul. Christ as the ultimate remnant-embodiment (Isaiah 4:2-6 — "the Branch of the LORD" is the remnant's glory) is the one remaining object of ultimate value when all else has been taken to Babylon.
22: "They will be taken to Babylon and remain there until the day when I attend to them, declares the LORD. Then I will bring them back and restore them to this place." The exile-and-return promise for the sacred vessels. Acts 3:21 — "heaven must receive him until the time comes for God to restore everything, as he promised long ago through his holy prophets." The restoration of the temple vessels anticipated in Jeremiah 27:22 points beyond Ezra's partial return (Ezra 1:7-11 — Cyrus restoring the vessels) to the ultimate restoration of all things that Christ will bring at his return. He is both the vessel taken away (Colossians 2:9 — all the fullness of deity in bodily form) and the one who brings back everything that was lost.
